title: The Bible as an Imperial Script
subtitle: A cleaned reading of Secret History #19
voice_policy: Direct argumentative prose derived from the lecture. Avoid repeated third-person framing. Do not impersonate first-person authorship except when quoting verified source material.
trace_policy: Paragraph-level transcript refs. Predictions and high-stakes claims stay tied to exact dated source spans.
dek: A source-grounded reading of Jiang's claim that Jewish identity is not treated here as simple continuity from ancient Israel, but as a Persian imperial construction: a Bible-shaped, temple-centered, purity-bound people made to stabilize and destabilize the Levant, then reused as a script by Britain, America, and Israel.
thesis: The lecture begins with a forecast: the American Empire will fall, and what replaces it is Israel, or Pax Judaica. To make that forecast plausible, Jiang builds a method. History should be read through recurring patterns, actor strategy, and religious eschatology. Sacred texts reveal not only what a people remembers but what it wants history to become. From there, Ezra and Nehemiah become the hinge. Persia does not merely free exiles out of mercy. It returns a small, text-bound, purity-bound community to Jerusalem so the Levant becomes governable through conflict. The modern claim is that later empires repeat the form: Britain writes a Balfour Declaration like an Artaxerxes letter, America manages Middle Eastern conflict like Persia, and Israel eventually seeks sovereignty from empire by becoming the dominant theocratic power of the region.

opening.heading: Core Reading
opening.text: This is not a lecture about the Jews as an unbroken ethnic essence. It is a lecture about identity as imperial technology. The people can remain, while the identity changes. The Bible can preserve memory, while also becoming the machine that edits memory. Persia needs the Levant divided; so it authorizes return, temple, law, and purity. The returning exiles become true Israel, the people who stayed become false or unclean, and Jerusalem becomes a managed conflict zone. Once that mechanism is visible, Jiang reads modern Israel through the same pattern: sacred text as strategy, return as policy, and sovereignty as the coming break with the empire that currently manages the conflict.

marks.text: identity as imperial technology
The people can remain, while the identity changes.
sacred text as strategy
sovereignty as the coming break with the empire that currently manages the conflict

beats.heading: The Method Before Israel
beats.summary: The lecture opens with Pax Judaica, then gives the method: patterns, game theory, and sacred texts as maps of historical desire.

paragraphs.text: The first sentence of the argument is already a forecast. The next ten classes will show how the American Empire falls, and what replaces it is Israel, named here as Pax Judaica. Ancient history is therefore not background. It is the long route to a future claim.

marks.text: what replaces it is Israel, named here as Pax Judaica

paragraphs.text: The method is called predictive history. A historical event is tested against larger patterns; actors are read through strategy, interests, and worldview; sacred texts are read as eschatological programs. The Bible and the Quran are not only beliefs. They show how a people imagines history should develop, and that makes them strategic documents.

marks.text: The Bible and the Quran are not only beliefs.
how a people imagines history should develop

paragraphs.text: The central thesis then arrives cleanly: Israel and Jewish identity are constructs of imperial imagination. The location matters because Jerusalem and the Levant are useful to empires. The identity matters because an empire can use a people's memory, law, temple, and desire to make the region serve imperial policy.

marks.text: constructs of imperial imagination
Jerusalem and the Levant are useful to empires

beats.heading: War As A Status-Quo Game
beats.summary: Before Persia, Jiang builds a general model of conquest: elite overproduction creates ritualized war until an outsider ignores the rules.

paragraphs.text: Conflict begins inside states. Too many elites chase too few positions, loyalties shift, and war can become a way to preserve equilibrium rather than destroy an enemy. In that world, elite violence is regulated. The point is not victory; the point is keeping the game alive.

marks.text: Too many elites chase too few positions
war can become a way to preserve equilibrium

paragraphs.text: That is why Jiang attacks The Art of War as advice for a controlled game. Real conquest begins when someone stops respecting the rules. Total warfare means promoting talent, feeding soldiers, destroying supply lines, attacking civilian capacity, and even killing the enemy's gods. The sacred rule of Mesopotamian city-states was that temples were off limits. The empire-maker sees that all the gold is in the temple.

marks.text: The empire-maker sees that all the gold is in the temple.

paragraphs.text: The repeated pattern is the outsider who does not respect the old game: Sargon, Qin, the lower noble, the mercenary. This is the bridge to Persia. Cyrus is not just another conqueror. He adds a different imperial technology: Zoroastrian trust, moral administration, mercy, co-optation of local elites, communication networks, and divide-and-rule.

marks.text: the outsider who does not respect the old game
divide-and-rule

beats.heading: The Levant Is The Control Lever
beats.summary: Jerusalem matters because the Levant links Egypt, Mesopotamia, and Anatolia; Persian policy turns exile and return into a tool for controlling that hinge.

paragraphs.text: The Levant matters because it touches the three great wealth zones: Egypt, Mesopotamia, and Anatolia. Whoever controls it can threaten Egypt. Whoever controls Egypt, in Jiang's compressed formula, controls the world. Jerusalem becomes center of the world not only because of religion, but because of trade and geopolitical leverage.

marks.text: Jerusalem becomes center of the world not only because of religion
Whoever controls Egypt controls the world.

paragraphs.text: After Solomon, the old Israelite world fractures. The northern kingdom falls, Judah is pulled by empires, Babylon takes hostages, and eventually the temple burns. The people are still around, but the identity of the Israelites is gone. That distinction is crucial: continuity of bodies is not continuity of identity.

marks.text: The people are still around, but the identity of the Israelites is gone.

paragraphs.text: The Bible says Cyrus returns the exiles because God commands him. Jiang does not accept that as a historical explanation. Mercy, righteousness, or grateful supporters may explain part of it, but the best explanation is divide-and-rule. Persia needs the Levant controlled, so it returns a small exile community whose loyalty, law, and conflict with locals make the region governable.

marks.text: the best explanation is divide-and-rule
a small exile community whose loyalty, law, and conflict with locals make the region governable

beats.heading: True Israel And False Israel
beats.summary: Ezra and Nehemiah show the mechanics: returnees claim purity, exclude locals from the temple, receive imperial authorization, and remake memory through scripture.

paragraphs.text: The returning exiles do not simply come home. They come back and say: we are the true Israelites, you are the false Israelites. The people who stayed may have remained in the land, but they married, worshiped, and remembered differently. That difference becomes the border of a new people.

marks.text: we are the true Israelites, you are the false Israelites
That difference becomes the border of a new people.

paragraphs.text: The temple scene carries the double meaning. Rebuilding the Second Temple is the happiest moment in Jewish history, but it also creates conflict with locals who say they worship the same God and should build too. For Jiang, that conflict is not a problem for Persia. It is the point.

marks.text: the happiest moment in Jewish history
that conflict is not a problem for Persia. It is the point.

paragraphs.text: Ezra then becomes the priest-intellectual of the new identity. He is treated as the creator of the Bible we have today, and Artaxerxes' authorization becomes the ancient template for later imperial letters. When Jiang compares it to the Balfour Declaration, the claim is not only analogy. The Bible becomes a script that later empires know how to reuse.

marks.text: the creator of the Bible we have today
The Bible becomes a script that later empires know how to reuse.

beats.heading: Purity, Law, And Memory
beats.summary: The new identity is stabilized through obedience to Yahweh, blood purity, Sabbath, interest rules, and the Bible as portable memory.

paragraphs.text: Ezra explains exile as punishment: the Israelites worshiped false gods, Yahweh sent prophets, and Babylon became the lesson. Return is therefore not restoration to the old religion. It is a second chance to prove loyalty through exclusive worship. Jiang names this the beginning of Jewish monotheism, influenced by Zoroastrianism, and distinct from the older Israelite polytheistic world.

marks.text: Babylon became the lesson
not restoration to the old religion

paragraphs.text: Purity then hardens into social law. The remaining Israelites become unclean because they worship foreign gods. Marriage becomes the border: do not give daughters to their sons, do not take their daughters, divorce foreign wives. Ezra rejects the Israelite heritage and accepts a new heritage based on blood purity and monotheistic devotion to Yahweh.

marks.text: Marriage becomes the border
a new heritage based on blood purity and monotheistic devotion to Yahweh

paragraphs.text: Nehemiah adds administration. The governor knows how empire works: the king does not tell you what he wants; you guess his intention and perform it as your own desire. He builds walls that provoke locals, repairs internal inequality, prohibits interest among Jews, and helps produce a community unified enough to lend internally without interest.

marks.text: the king does not tell you what he wants; you guess his intention
lend internally without interest

paragraphs.text: The last step is memory. The people gather and hear the law. Who are we? What do we remember? The answer is whatever is written in the Bible. Ezra combines documents into the book, and that book makes the identity resilient because memory can travel even when political life collapses.

marks.text: Who are we? What do we remember? The answer is whatever is written in the Bible.
memory can travel even when political life collapses

beats.heading: Predictions From Scripture
beats.summary: The final movement converts the biblical model into predictions about the Third Temple, Israeli theocracy, American-Israeli rupture, and Palestinian displacement.
beats.source_note: This beat preserves Jiang's dated 2025-11-18 predictions and high-stakes geopolitical claims as source-grounded lecture claims, not as independently verified event history.

paragraphs.text: Once Bible and history are connected, Jiang says prediction becomes possible. The first prediction is the Third Temple: Israel will rebuild the Temple of Solomon by destroying Al-Aqsa Mosque, because the biblical memory of temple restoration is central to Israeli identity. The result, in his forecast, is conflict with the Islamic world.

marks.text: prediction becomes possible
Israel will rebuild the Temple of Solomon by destroying Al-Aqsa Mosque

paragraphs.text: The second prediction is theocracy. If the biblical lesson is that loss of Jerusalem came from offending God, then keeping Israel means obeying God's law. Tel Aviv may appear liberal, open, and cosmopolitan, but Jiang says that is temporary: Israel becomes more and more a state where the Bible is the law.

marks.text: Tel Aviv may appear liberal, open, and cosmopolitan, but Jiang says that is temporary
the Bible is the law

paragraphs.text: The third prediction is expansion. As Pax Judaica grows, more Jews return, and no regional power can stop Israel from expanding. The same identity remains fluid, flexible, and open because it was created by empire for empire; the difference is that Israel now wants to stop being managed by another empire.

marks.text: fluid, flexible, and open because it was created by empire for empire
Israel now wants to stop being managed by another empire

paragraphs.text: The student question at the end forces the modern transfer into view. Persia kept the Levant divided so everyone needed Persia to manage the conflict. America, in Jiang's answer, plays the same role today: Israel and surrounding states all require American mediation. But Israel resents that position. The coming break is described as a divorce between America and Israel, and Jiang says Israel wins.

marks.text: America plays the same role today
a divorce between America and Israel

paragraphs.text: The last clarification matters for public reading. Jiang stops the room when a student says Jews are attacking Palestinians. Do not say the Jews, he says. Israelites, Jews, and Israelis are different categories. The state actors are Israelis. Only after that distinction does he give the stark closing forecast: Israel clears the West Bank and Gaza of Palestinians to establish a theocratic state.

marks.text: Israelites, Jews, and Israelis are different categories.
Israel clears the West Bank and Gaza of Palestinians to establish a theocratic state

questions.question: How does Persian control of Jerusalem transfer to modern-day society and current conflicts?
questions.answer: The answer is that Persia controlled the Levant by creating a conflict-producing entity that both enemies and allies needed Persia to manage. Jiang says America now occupies the same imperial-manager role in the Middle East: Israel and surrounding states remain in conflict, and all require the United States to mediate. Israel, in his forecast, will eventually reject that dependency and win a divorce from American empire.

questions.question: Did Jews stop attacking Palestinians? Why has the news about Israeli attacks disappeared?
questions.answer: Jiang first corrects the category: do not say Jews when the claim concerns Israeli state action. Israelites, Jews, and Israelis are different. Jews are people who practice Judaism and include a diaspora, including many who oppose Israel. Israelis are citizens of the nation of Israel. After that correction, he answers with a prediction: Israel will clear the West Bank and Gaza of Palestinians to establish a completely Jewish theocratic state, though the process will take time.

source_note: The transcript contains student readings from Ezra, Nehemiah, and the Balfour Declaration. Those readings are treated here as quoted source material inside the lecture, while Jiang's interpretations and predictions are tied to his answer spans. Several proper nouns are ASR-damaged; public prose uses corrected common names where context is clear, such as Al-Aqsa Mosque.
archive_note: This page is the public reading surface: a compressed argument with paragraph-level source refs and selective marks. The full transcript remains available as the audit view, and the compiled JSON remains available for downstream corpus work.
